NTLM

NTLM is Microsoft's legacy authentication protocol used to verify the identity of users and computers within Windows environments. It performs a challenge-response authentication process without transmitting the user's password over the network. Although largely replaced by Kerberos in Active Directory domains, NTLM is still supported for compatibility with legacy systems and specific authentication scenarios.

Objectives

  • Authenticate users without sending plaintext passwords.
  • Support authentication for legacy systems and applications.
  • Provide fallback authentication when Kerberos is unavailable.

Authentication Process

  1. The client requests authentication.
  2. The server generates and sends a random challenge (nonce).
  3. The client encrypts the challenge using a value derived from the user's NT password hash.
  4. The server forwards the response to the Domain Controller, or validates it locally for local accounts.
  5. The Domain Controller verifies the response using the stored NT hash.
  6. If the values match, authentication succeeds.

NTLM Versions

LM (LAN Manager)

  • Original authentication protocol.
  • Weak cryptography.
  • Considered obsolete and insecure.

NTLM

  • Improved over LM.
  • Uses the NT password hash.
  • No mutual authentication.

NTLMv2

  • Current and most secure NTLM version.
  • Uses stronger cryptographic mechanisms.
  • Includes client and server challenges to improve security.
  • Still less secure than Kerberos.

Common Use Cases

  • Legacy Windows applications.
  • Standalone, non-domain systems.
  • Cross-domain authentication without trust relationships.
  • Fallback when Kerberos authentication fails.
  • Accessing resources by IP address instead of hostname.

Limitations

  • Provides no native mutual authentication.
  • Relies on password hashes.
  • More vulnerable to credential relay attacks than Kerberos.
  • Less efficient and less secure than Kerberos.
  • Does not support modern delegation features.

Common Attacks

  • Pass-the-Hash (PtH)
  • NTLM Relay
  • Credential Capture
  • Brute Force / Password Spraying
  • SMB Relay attacks

Best Practices

  • Prefer Kerberos whenever possible.
  • Disable LM and NTLMv1.
  • Restrict or disable NTLM where legacy compatibility is not required.
  • Enable SMB signing and Extended Protection where applicable.
  • Monitor and audit NTLM authentication events.
  • Enforce strong password policies and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) where possible.
